coredata.dc:title: Preparation of species-specific monoclonal antibody and development of fluorescence immunoassay based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer of carbon dots for accurate and sensitive detection of Alicyclobacillus acidoterrestris in apple juice
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Yiheng
Sun, Yuhan
Qu, Xiaowei
Zhou, Lei
Yue, Tianli
Yuan, Yahong
coredata.dc:description: The growth and metabolism of Alicyclobacillus acidoterrestris can lead to the spoilage of commercial fruit juice. Existing methods have some drawbacks such as complex sample pretreatment, skilled technician requirement, reduced sensitivity and specificity. Herein, a novel fluorescence immunoassay was developed using a monoclonal antibody (mAb) against A. acidoterrestris as the sensing element and carbon dots (CDs) as the signal response unit. The CDs can be quenched via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) by the oxidization product of p-phenylenediamine (PPD), a chromogenic substrate of horseradish peroxidase (HRP). This approach showed enhanced accuracy and sensitivity with relatively low limit of detection (LOD) of 6.16 × 102 CFU mL−1. Moreover, apple juice contaminated with 1 CFU mL−1 of A. acidoterrestris can be identified after 24 h enrichment. This fluorescence immunoassay could serve as a powerful tool for laboratory identification and on-site inspection of A. acidoterrestris, reducing the adverse effect on the quality of fruit juice.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Alicyclobacillus acidoterrestris
Monoclonal antibody
Carbon dots
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Fluorescence immunoassay

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of mAb against Hp00440
Establishment of fluorescence ELISA for A. acidoterrestris
Detection of A. acidoterrestris in real samples
Feasibility of CDs based fluorescence immunoassay
Detection of A. acidoterrestris in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM and HRTEM (inset) image of CDs. (B) FT-IR of CDs. (C) XPS of CDs. (D) UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs. Inset: photographs of CDs solution under (a) daylight and (b) 365 nm UV light.

(A) Construction of recombinant plasmid pET-28a (+)-N007_00440. Lane 1, PCR product of N007_00440. Lane 2, fragments of pET-28a (+)-N007_00440 digested with Nco I and Xho I. M1, DNA marker 2000. M2, DNA marker 5000. (B) Preparation and characterization of fusion Hp00440. Lane 1 and lane 2, protein pattern under induction and uninduction, respectively. Lane 3 and lane 5, cell wall proteins of A. acidoterrestris DSM 3923 identified by SDS-PAGE and western blot, respectively. Lane 4 and lane 6 are purified fusion Hp00440 detected by SDS-PAGE and western blot, respectively.

(A) Absorption spectra of sole PPD and in the presence HRP or H2O2 or HRP/H2O2. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs in different solutions. Inset: photographs of CDs solution under 365 nm UV light in the (a) absence and (b) presence of PPDox. (C) Absorption spectra of PPD and PPDox and fluorescence emission spectra of CDs. (D) Fluorescence lifetime of CDs in the absence and presence of PPDox.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs in the presence of different concentrations of A. acidoterrestris (0, 101, 102, 103, 104, 0.5 × 105, 105, 0.5 × 106, 106 CFU mL−1). (B) Change of I/I0 at 510 nm in the presence of different concentrations of A. acidoterrestris from 0 to 106 CFU mL−1. Inset: Corresponding plot of I/I0 versus the logarithm of bacteria concentration. (C) Specificity of the developed fluorescence ELISA. (D) Performance of the fluorescence ELISA on the identification of A. acidoterrestris in apple juice at different initial concentrations.
Comparison of different immunoassays for the detection of A. acidoterrestris.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Cross-reactivity of prepared antibody with other bacteria species.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of A. acidoterrestris in apple juice (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Preparation of species-specific monoclonal antibody and development of fluorescence immunoassay based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer of carbon dots for accurate and sensitive detection of Alicyclobacillus acidoterrestris in apple juice

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University, Yangling 712100, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The growth and metabolism of Alicyclobacillus acidoterrestris can lead to the spoilage of commercial fruit juice. Existing methods have some drawbacks such as complex sample pretreatment, skilled technician requirement, reduced sensitivity and specificity. Herein, a novel fluorescence immunoassay was developed using a monoclonal antibody (mAb) against A. acidoterrestris as the sensing element and carbon dots (CDs) as the signal response unit. The CDs can be quenched via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) by the oxidization product of p-phenylenediamine (PPD), a chromogenic substrate of horseradish peroxidase (HRP). This approach showed enhanced accuracy and sensitivity with relatively low limit of detection (LOD) of 6.16 × 102 CFU mL−1. Moreover, apple juice contaminated with 1 CFU mL−1 of A. acidoterrestris can be identified after 24 h enrichment. This fluorescence immunoassay could serve as a powerful tool for laboratory identification and on-site inspection of A. acidoterrestris, reducing the adverse effect on the quality of fruit juice.

ce:section.ce:para: Alicyclobacillus acidoterrestris is a thermoacidophilic, spore-forming bacterium with a wide temperature (20–65 °C) and pH (2.0–6.5) growth range (Smit, Cameron, Venter et al., 2011). The spore can resist thermal treatment up to 90 °C for 16–23 min and germinate in suitable environment (Molva & Baysal, 2015). Moreover, the high ω-alicyclic fatty acid content in the cell membrane can protect A. acidoterrestris from heat above the growth temperature range (Kannenberg et al., 1984). Thus, these two characteristics endow A. acidoterrestris with the ability to survive in the pasteurization treatment common applied in the fruit juice process. The growth of A. acidoterrestris can cause flat-sour spoilage without gas production and pH alteration and negligible sediment in fruit juice, which is difficult to identify visually (Sinigaglia et al., 2003). Moreover, guaiacol can be produced by A. acidoterrestris using vanillin as precursor when the cell concentration exceeds 105 CFU mL−1 (Witthuhn, van der Merwe, Venter et al., 2012). This leads to a phenolic, medicinal, or disinfectant-like smell in the juice. Consequently, contamination by A. acidoterrestris can severely affect fruit juice quality, resulting in bad reputation and pecuniary loss to the manufacturer.

Plate counting is a common conventional method to detect A. acidoterrestris because it is cheap and accurate. However, this method always requires cumbersome sample pre-treatment and takes 3–5 days, which greatly reduced the efficiency of A. acidoterrestris identification (Chang & Kang, 2004). Therefore, many attempts have been devoted to address the inefficiency of traditional method. And up to now, some novel techniques such as loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) (Chen et al., 2011), and single primer isothermal amplification (SPIA) (Yang et al., 2017) have been established. These techniques can identify A. acidoterrestris accurately and sensitively in real sample with relatively low limit of detection (LOD). However, the need for expensive equipment and the high possibility of false positives makes these tools unsuitable for practical applications. Other methods such as electronic nose (Concina et al., 2010) and gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) (Huang et al., 2015) have also been used to detect A. acidoterrestris. These methods can quickly and indirectly identify A. acidoterrestris by analyzing its off-flavor metabolites without complicated sample pre-treatment. However, the precise quantification of A. acidoterrestris is difficult to achieve due to the ambiguous relationship between cell number and metabolite concentration. And the requirement of sophisticated instruments and skilled technician has also hindered their widespread use.

Based on the high catalytic capacity of enzyme and specific recognition and high affinity between antigen and antibody, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELISA) has been a robust tool in qualitative and quantitative analysis of certain analyte since it’s developed (Nistor & Emneus, 2005). This analytical technique can easily distinguish positive samples in a high-throughput manner without the aid of advanced equipment, providing a simple and accurate way for food quality control (Nguyen et al., 2019). Therefore, several types of ELISA have been successfully applied for the sensitive and specific detection of A. acidoterrestris (Li et al., 2014; Mast et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2012, 2013). Nonetheless, the relatively low signal-to-noise ratio has reduced the sensitivity of these assays. To solve such defect, a feasible strategy is to introduce fluorescence signal to the immunoassay system to improve the sensitivity (Barroso et al., 2016). Currently, most established fluorescence immunoassays employ fluorescent nanomaterials, such as quantum dots (Ensafi et al., 2016), upconversion nanoparticles (Guo et al., 2020), semiconductor nanoparticles (Akram et al., 2020), and polymer dots (Yang et al., 2020), as labels of antibody to generate signals in the detection system. Despite the inherent advantages of these approaches, the cumbersome labelling steps can reduce the affinity of antibody to the target antigen, resulting in decreased sensitivity. In comparison, fluorescence immunoassays based on the label-free strategy is more promising for analyte detection.

Hence, in this study, we developed a novel label-free fluorescence immunoassay for the detection of A. acidoterrestris in apple juice. First, green emission carbon dots (CDs) were facilely prepared via a hydrothermal method. The fluorescence of the CDs could be quenched via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) by 2,5-diamino-N, N′-bis(p-aminophenyl)-l,4-benzoquinone di-imine (PPDox), an oxidation product of PPD in the presence of H2O2 catalyzed by HRP. In consideration of the common application of HRP in immunoassay, a turn-off fluorescence ELISA was proposed herein. To precisely identify A. acidoterrestris, a monoclonal antibody (mAb) against this bacterium was prepared using the hypothetical protein N007_00440 (Hp00440, accession number:EPZ48219.1), a species-specific cell wall protein as the antigen (Shi, Yue, Zhang et al., 2018). Therefore, the sensitivity and specificity of the resulting fluorescence ELISA were greatly improved by combining the excellent properties of CDs and mAb. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first attempt to explore the feasibility of fluorescence ELISA in the detection of A. acidoterrestris. Moreover, this strategy also shows great potential in the detection of other contaminants in food matrices.

A. acidoterrestris DSM 3923, A. acidocaldarius DSM 446, A. acidiphilus DSM 14558, A. contaminans DSM 17975, A. cycloheptanicus DSM 4006, A. fastidiosus DSM 17978, A. herbarius DSM 13609, A. hesperidum DSM 12489, and A. pomorum DSM 14955 were obtained from Deutsche Sammlung von Mikroorganismen und Zellkulturen (DSMZ, Germany). Bacillus subtilis ATCC 6051, Brevibacillus brevis ATCC 8246, Lactobacillus acidophilus ATCC 4356, Lactobacillus plantarum ATCC 14917, and Staphylococcus aureus ATCC 12600 were purchased from American Type Culture Collection (ATCC, USA). PPD, ethylenediamine, and catechol were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Isopropyl-beta-d-thiogalactopyranoside (IPTG), HRP, and HRP-conjugated mouse anti-rabbit IgG (HRP-Ab) were purchased from Sangon Biotech (Shanghai, China). E. coli BL21 (DE3) competent cells, pET-28a (+) vector, Pierce™ ECL Plus Western Blotting Substrate, Pierce™ Protein G Plus Agarose, and Pierce™ Rapid Antibody Isotyping Kit-Mouse were purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific (USA). Gel Extraction Kit, Bacterial DNA Kit, Plasmid Mini Kit were purchased from Omega BIO-TEK (USA). Premix Taq, DNA marker, Protein Marker, QuickCut Xho I, QuickCut Nco I, and T4 DNA ligase were purchased from Takara Biotechnology (Beijing, China). Ni-NTA His Bind Resin, and polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF) membrane were purchased from Merck Millipore (USA). Polyclonal antibody (pAb) against A. acidoterrestris DSM 3923 was prepared by our group previously (Shi et al., 2018). Concentrated apple juice purchased from Haisheng Fresh Fruit Juice Co., Ltd (Shaanxi, China) was diluted to ca. 15 °Brix and sterilized at 121 °C for 15 min for further use. Primers used for the amplification of N007_00440 (coding gene of Hp00440) are listed in Table S1.

The morphology of CDs was characterized by JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out on ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (ThermoFisher Scientific, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra was recorded on a Vetex 70 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). UV–vis absorption spectra were collected on a UV-2550 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were obtained from a F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan). Fluorescence lifetime was measured by an FLS 980 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK).

CDs were prepared by the hydrothermal method according to the literature with some modifications (Li et al., 2016). Briefly, 0.2753 g of catechol was dissolved in 25 mL ultrapure water and then 300 μL of ethylenediamine was added. The solution was mixed under ultrasound for 10 min and then transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After the reaction, the autoclave was naturally cooled to room temperature. The resultant solution was diluted once with ultrapure water and centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 15 min to remove large particles. The supernatant was collected and filtered through a 0.22-μm filter member. Finally, the solution was dialyzed against ultrapure water using a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut of 500 Da) for 48 h. The solid CDs were obtained by freeze-drying.

The genomic DNA of A. acidoterrestris DSM 3923 was extracted and used as template for the amplification of N007_00440. The PCR products were then recycled and purified. After being digested by Nco I and Xho I, the resultant fragments were cloned into the Nco I/Xho I site of pET-28a (+) vector. The recombinant expression plasmid was designated as pET-28a (+)-00440 after being verified by DNA sequencing. Next, the recombinant plasmid was transformed into E. coli BL21 (DE3) competent cells. The positive clone was picked and induced by IPTG at 20 °C for 12 h for the overexpression of fusion protein. Finally, the fusion protein Hp00440 was purified using Ni-NTA His Bind Resin and stored at −20 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of mAb against Hp00440
ce:section.ce:para: In the first immunization, 50 μg of purified fusion protein Hp00440 emulsified with Freund’s complete adjuvant was injected intraperitoneally into BALB/c female mice (6–8 weeks old). After two weeks, three boost immunizations were performed every two weeks using the same amount of protein emulsified with Freund’s incomplete adjuvant. The final boost immunization was performed using 50 μg of unemulsified protein, and the mice were killed three days later. The splenocytes were then collected and fused with myeloma cells SP2/0. Positive hybridoma cells were selected and sub-cloned three times by limiting dilution. For massive production of mAb, hybridoma cells were injected into the abdominal cavity of mouse. After ten days, the ascites containing mAb were collected and purified using Protein G Plus Agarose according to the manufacturer’s instructions. The isotype and titer of mAb was also determined. All animal experiments were conducted in accordance with the recommendations of the Animal Ethics Procedures and Guidelines of the People’s Republic of China and approved by the Ethics Committee of Northwest A&F University.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of fluorescence ELISA for A. acidoterrestris
ce:section.ce:para: Each well of a 96-well microplate was coated with 100 μL of mAb (2.5 μg mL−1), diluted with carbonate buffer (0.05 M, pH 9.6), and incubated at 4 °C overnight. The plate was then washed with 200 μL PBST (0.01 M PBS (pH 7.4) containing 0.05% (v/v) Tween-20) three times and blocked with PBST containing 5% (w/v) skim milk (200 μL/well) at 37 °C for 2 h. Excess skim milk in the well was removed by washing with PBST. Next, 100 μL of diluted apple juice containing A. acidoterrestris cells was added to the well and incubated at 37 °C for 1 h. The well was then washed three times with PBST to remove unbounded cells. Next, 100 μL of pAb (1.25 μg mL−1) diluted with PBST was added to each well and incubated at 37 °C for 1 h. Following another wash with PBST, 100 μL of HRP-Ab (1:4000 diluted with PBST) was added to the well and incubated at 37 °C for 1 h. The plate was rinsed again with PBST three times, and 100 μL of phosphate buffer (0.02 M, pH 6.0) containing PPD (1 mM) and H2O2 (2 mM) was added to the well and kept at room temperature for 30 min. Finally, 10 μL of CDs (1 mg mL−1) was introduced, and the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded under 410 nm excitation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of A. acidoterrestris in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The diluted apple juices were inoculated with A. acidoterrestris DSM 3923 at the concentration of 1, 10, 100 CFU mL−1, respectively. Then, the juice samples were cultured at 45 °C for 36 h with continuous shaking. Some of the cultures were sampled every 2 h and used for the detection of A. acidoterrestris in accordance with the established fluorescence immunoassay procedures.

The CDs were facilely synthesized via a solvothermal method using the catechol and ethylenediamine as precursors. The morphology of the as-prepared CDs was observed by TEM. As presented in Fig. 1 A, the CDs were uniformly spherical in shape and well dispersed in aqueous solution. The particle size was narrowly distributed in the range of 4–7 nm with the average size of 5.37 nm (Fig. S1). The high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (inset of Fig. 1A) revealed that the CDs had a typical graphite-like crystalline structure with a clear interplanar lattice fringe spacing of 0.21 nm, which is consistent with the (100) facet of graphite carbon (Tang et al., 2014).

The surface functional groups of CDs were investigated by FT-IR. As shown in Fig. 1B, the wide absorption band at 3200–3500 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration of OH/NH (Liu et al., 2017), and the absorption peak at 2922 cm−1 could be attributed to the stretching vibration of CH (Wang et al., 2018). The absorption band centered at 1639 cm−1 originates from the stretching vibration of CO (Yu et al., 2019). The narrow band at 1360–1400 cm−1 represents the characteristic stretching vibration of CN (Lu et al., 2017). In addition, the absorption band at 980–1200 cm−1 is associated with the bending vibration of CO (Ding et al., 2016). These results indicated that the as-prepared CDs are particularly rich in amino and hydroxy functional groups on the surface, endowing the CDs with excellent water solubility.

Meanwhile, XPS measurements were performed to further investigate the surface state and elemental composition of CDs. As shown in Fig. 1C, the full scan spectra reveal that the CDs mainly consist of three elements of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen with three main peaks centered at 284.7, 399.1, and 531.1 eV corresponding to the binding energy of C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively. The high-resolution spectra of C 1 s (Fig. S2A) shows five peaks at 284.2, 284.6, 285.6, 286.3, and 287.6 eV relating to carbon in the state of CC (sp2), CC (sp3), CN (sp3), CO (sp2), and CO (sp2), respectively (Song et al., 2017). The N 1 s spectra (Fig. S2B) can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 399.1 eV and 400.4 eV, which are associated with CN and NH bonds, respectively (Yue et al., 2019). This implies that nitrogen has been doped into the synthesized CDs. Meanwhile, the two peaks divided from the O 1 s spectra (Fig. S2C) at 530.7 eV and 532.0 eV are ascribed to the CO and CO bonds (Hou, Wang, Zhou et al., 2016), respectively, suggesting the existence of oxygen groups on the surface of CDs. The information concluded from the XPS analysis are good in accordance with the results of FT-IR, indicating the presence of high amount of hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of the as-synthesized CDs.

The optical properties of CDs, including UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation, and emission spectra, were also investigated. As shown in Fig. 1D, the UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs exhibit a characteristic peak at 410 nm. This wide absorption band is ascribed to the n-π* transitions by the surface states due to the low energy absorption of functional groups on the surface of CDs (Hou et al., 2016). In the fluorescence spectra, the maximum excitation wavelength is also located at 410 nm. Under this condition, the CDs can be efficiently excited with a maximum emission peak centered at 510 nm. Moreover, the bright green fluorescence of CDs aqueous solution could be observed under 365 nm UV light. Compared with the absorption property, this result demonstrates that the fluorescence emission of CDs originates from the absorption in a low energy region. In addition, the stability of CDs at different pH values was also tested. As shown in Fig. S3, the fluorescence of CDs almost disappeared in strong acid environment and gradually recovered as the pH increased from 2 to 7. While the fluorescence intensity slightly decreased in the alkaline environment. These pH-responsive features could be ascribed to the protonation and deprotonation of functional groups on the surface of CDs (Hou et al., 2013).

Owing to the complexity of bacteria cell, some components like teichoic acid and protein homologues coexisting in different species could serve as common antigens to elicit immunoreaction (van Dalen et al., 2020). This could lead to low specificity of the antibody against the entire cell. Thus, false positive results often occur in ELISA system for bacteria detection. Thus, Hp00440, a cell wall protein of A. acidoterrestris proven to be a species-specific biomarker with immunogenicity (Shi et al., 2018), was chosen as an antigen for the generation of high specificity monoclonal antibody. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the coding sequence of Hp00440, N007_00440 was successfully amplified with the length of 1722 bp, which is consistent with the predicted size (lane 1). Then, N007_00440 was cloned into the pET-28a (+) vector. As shown in Fig. 2A, a large and a small band were clearly observed (lane 2), corresponding to the linearized pET-28a (+) and N007_00440, respectively. This result demonstrated that the recombinant plasmid pET-28a (+)-N007_00440 was successfully constructed, which was also confirmed by DNA sequencing (data not shown).

Next, the recombinant plasmid pET-28a (+)-N007_00440 was transferred into E. coli BL21 (DE3)-competent cells, and the overexpression of fusion Hp00440 was induced by IPTG. As exhibited in Fig. 2B, a wide deep color band could be clearly observed at around 75 kDa (lane 1), whereas no such band was observed in uninduced sample (lane 2), indicating successful expression of fusion Hp00440. Then, the fusion protein was purified before using it for mice immunization. The single band (lane 4) shown in Fig. 2B is at the correct location without any other bands present, suggesting high purity of the resulting products.

The mAb was then prepared using the purified fusion protein as the antigen. As shown in Fig. S4, two bands can be obviously observed after purification that the large band at around 47 kDa represents the heavy chain of mAb and the small one with the size of about 26 kDa is the light chain. The isotype and titer of mAb were determined to be IgG1-kappa and 1:80000, respectively (data not shown). Furthermore, western blot analysis was performed to confirm the reactivity of the prepared mAb with fusion Hp00440 and cell wall proteins of A. acidoterrestris. Cell wall proteins were extracted as described previously (Shi et al., 2018). As shown in Fig. 2B, strong reaction between mAb and protein samples with two wide deep black bands present in lane 5 and lane 6, respectively. Compared with the cell wall proteins profile (lane 3), the probable location of the original Hp00440 was marked with red arrows. The molecular weight of fusion Hp00440 is larger than the original. Such a difference may be derived from the histidine tag in the fusion protein, making the increase of molecular weight. In conclusion, the prepared mAb could react with the cell wall of A. acidoterrestris with high affinity and specificity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of CDs based fluorescence immunoassay
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 3 A, the PPD solution (pH 6.0) remained transparent with no absorption peak at 400–800 nm. Meanwhile, the addition of HRP or H2O2 can hardly interfere with the absorption spectra of PPD. In contrast, the absorbance of the PPD solution was significantly enhanced with a characteristic absorption peak centered at 530 nm when HRP and H2O2 were added together to the system. This was accompanied by a color change from clear to reddish brown, suggesting that only when HRP and H2O2 were simultaneously exist in the solution can PPD be efficiently oxidized to PPDox. Such a transformation was easily achieved in weak acid and neutral environment (Fig. S5A), which is consistent with the previous study (Jiao et al., 2000). Moreover, the oxidation reaction could be completed within 30 min under the most suitable pH of 6.0 (Fig. S5B).

In consideration of the fluorescence properties of CDs, the absorption spectra of PPDox could greatly overlap with its emission spectra (Fig. 3C), offering a possibility for fluorescence quenching. As shown in Fig. 3B, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was barely affected by sole PPD, HRP, or H2O2. Similarly, the emission spectra of CDs remained unchanged in a mixture of PPD/HRP or PPD/H2O2 or HRP/H2O2. On the contrary, significant decrease of fluorescence intensity was induced by simultaneously adding PPD, HRP, and H2O2 to the CDs solution. This indicated that the formation of PPDox do cause the fluorescence quenching of CDs. Owing to the highly overlap between the absorption spectra of PPDox and the emission spectra of CDs, the possible mechanism for this quenching phenomenon could ascribe to the FRET or inner filter effect (IFE) (Yan et al., 2018). At the same time, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs decreased from 7.2 ns to 5.7 ns in the presence of PPDox (Fig. 3D). As the fluorescence lifetime is hardly affected by other substances in the IFE, therefore, the quenching mechanism could be attributed to the FRET. Furthermore, the small size of CDs could fulfill the distance requirement of less than 10 nm of FRET (Morla-Folch et al., 2020). In view of the common application of HRP in ELISA, thus, a fluorescence ELISA could be established for the detection of A. acidoterrestris.

In ELISA system, the cut-off value is the essential criterion to distinguish between positive and negative (Asensio et al., 2008). The validity of results also rests with the cut-off value of ELISA. Therefore, before the detection of A. acidoterrestris was performed by employing the established fluorescence ELISA, the cut-off value of this assay was determined. Forty-five negative samples (non-A. acidoterrestris) were analyzed, and the fluorescence intensity ratio of I/I0 at 510 nm (where I and I0 represent the fluorescence intensities of the detection system in the presence and absence of bacteria, respectively) was recorded. The cut-off value was defined as the mean value (E) minus three times the standard deviation (SD). As shown in Fig. S6, the E and SD were 0.9521 and 0.0228, respectively. Thus, the cut-off value of this assay was calculated to be 0.8837. Samples can be determined as positive when I/I0 at 510 nm is below the cut-off value.

To evaluate the specificity of the proposed fluorescence ELISA, different bacteria were tested using this assay. As shown in Fig. 4C, all species except for A. acidoterrestris showed little influence on the fluorescence intensity of CDs that I/I0 of these samples were all greater than the cut-off value even at the high cell concentration of 1.0 × 106 CFU mL−1. Notably, species belonging to the same genus were also determined as negative. It is demonstrated that the established approach is highly selective in the identification of A. acidoterrestris. As A. acidoterrestris represents the main species of genus Alicyclobacillus causing the deterioration of fruit juice and others have little chance to involve in the spoilage incident (Smit et al., 2011). The excellent specificity further proved that the constructed fluorescence ELISA holds great possibility of recognizing the contamination in fruit juice by A. acidoterrestris timely and accurately.

The intra-assay and inter-assay variations were tested by evaluating the reproducibility and precision of the developed fluorescence ELISA. As shown in Table 2 , the average recoveries range from 96.9% to 103.5% with a relative low coefficient of variation (CV) of 1.3–6.4%. The excellent repeatability indicates that the proposed assay is reliable for the detection of A. acidoterrestris.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of A. acidoterrestris in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The apple juices were artificially contaminated with A. acidoterrestris at different concentrations and used to assess the capability of the fluorescence ELISA in practical applications. Fig. 4D shows that the fluorescence intensity of the sensing system decreased dynamically with the growth of A. acidoterrestris. A positive result can be detected after 16 h of cultivation at 100 CFU mL−1. And the detection time was 18 h and 24 h when the concentration of A. acidoterrestris was 10 CFU mL−1 and 1 CFU mL−1, respectively. These results suggest the fluorescence ELISA could identify A. acidoterrestris in real samples.

In this work, in order to develop a novel method for the detection of A. acidoterrestris, the main microorganism causing the spoilage of fruit juice, a highly fluorescent CDs was prepared which could be efficiently quenched through FRET by PPDox. And a novel turn-off fluorescence ELISA for the detection of A. acidoterrestris was thus constructed using the prepared CDs as fluorescence response signal. Moreover, in order to enhance the specificity of the method, a mAb against the cell wall of A. acidoterrestris, exhibiting great reduced cross-reactivity with non-target species, was first prepared and applied in the sensing system. Consequently, owing to the advantages of cost-effective, convenience, rapidness, superior sensitivity and selectivity, the established approach is suitable for laboratory and manufactory use and could serve as a robust tool to prevent the occurrence of deterioration in fruit juice resulted from the proliferation and metabolism of A. acidoterrestris.

Yiheng Shi: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Yuhan Sun: Conceptualization, Investigation, Validation, Visualization, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Xiaowei Qu: Conceptualization, Validation, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Lei Zhou: Conceptualization, Validation, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Tianli Yue: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing - review & editing. Yahong Yuan: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31671866).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2021.129069.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
